title: Top K Frequent Elements
description: Given an integer array `nums` and an integer `k`, return *the* `k` *most frequent elements*. You may return the answer in **any order**.
whatShouldHappen: Count frequencies of all elements using a Hash Map
Use a Min-Heap to keep track of the top k elements
Alternatively, use Bucket Sort for an O(n) solution
skeletonHint: First, count the frequency of each number. Then, you can use a heap to find the k most frequent, or use 'bucket sort' where the index of the bucket is the frequency and the value is a list of numbers with that frequency.
patternExplanation: Hashing provides O(n) frequency counting. Using a heap allows us to find the top k in O(n log k), while bucket sort can achieve O(n) by using frequencies as array indices.

title: Find Median from Data Stream
description:
The **median** is the middle value in an ordered integer list. If the size of the list is even, there is no middle value, and the median is the mean of the two middle values.

Implement the MedianFinder class:

- `MedianFinder()` initializes the MedianFinder object.
- `void addNum(int num)` adds the integer `num` from the data stream to the data structure.
- `double findMedian()` returns the median of all elements so far.
whatShouldHappen: Maintain two heaps: a max-heap for the lower half and a min-heap for the upper half
Balance the heaps so their size difference is at most 1
Calculate the median from the tops of the heaps
skeletonHint: Use two heaps to divide the numbers into two halves. A Max-Heap for the smaller half and a Min-Heap for the larger half. When adding a number, first push it to one heap, then rebalance by moving the 'peak' element to the other. The median is either the peak of the larger heap or the average of both peaks.
patternExplanation: Two heaps allow us to efficiently track the middle elements of a dynamic data stream. Max-heap keeps the smaller half and Min-heap keeps the larger half, making median retrieval O(1) and insertion O(log n).
